title: Reviewing and Updating Estate Documents
summary: Estate documents need periodic review, not a one-time setup — life changes can quietly make old documents outdated.
category: Life Transitions III: Legal & Financial Shifts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Reviewing and Updating Estate Documents | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Estate documents need periodic review, not a one-time setup — life changes can quietly make old documents outdated.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Wills, POAs, and beneficiary designations should be reviewed after major life events.
Beneficiary designations on accounts often override what a will says.
A periodic review (every few years, or after major changes) catches most issues.

paragraphs: Marriages, divorces, deaths, and moves to a new state can all affect whether existing estate documents still reflect actual wishes — a will drafted decades ago may no longer match the current family situation or state's legal requirements.

One frequently overlooked detail: beneficiary designations on retirement accounts, life insurance, and similar accounts typically override what's written in a will, which means an outdated designation can undo careful estate planning without anyone realizing it until it's too late.

nextSteps: Review all estate documents after any major family change.
Check beneficiary designations specifically, not just the will itself.
Set a recurring reminder to review everything every few years even without a major change.
disclaimer: This article is general information, not legal advice, and laws vary by state. The specific requirements, deadlines, and procedures described here may not apply exactly to your situation. Confirm all details with a licensed elder law attorney before acting, especially before signing, filing, or relying on any document.
